content: Silicon Valley has abandoned its historical mission of collaborating with government on world-changing technologies. Engineers now build advertising algorithms and food delivery apps instead of addressing urgent national challenges. The software industry must renew its commitment to addressing our most urgent challenges, including the new arms race of artificial intelligence.

AI begins to replace nuclear weapons as the foundation of military deterrence. The question is not whether a new generation of increasingly autonomous, AI-driven weapons will be built, but rather who will build them and for what purpose. America's rivals are racing ahead in this critical technology.

We need a new Manhattan Project for AI dominance. Silicon Valley must reunite with the defense establishment, Americans must recover a sense of national purpose, and the government must launch a coordinated national effort to ensure American dominance in military AI.

The rise of the West wasn't due to the superiority of its ideas or values or religion, but rather by its superiority in applying organized violence. Leaders must reject intellectual fragility and preserve space for ideological confrontation. A willingness to risk the disapproval of the crowd directly correlates with technological and economic outperformance.

The fundamental problem is technological agnostics — leaders who build things simply because they can, lacking larger collective purpose or authentic values. Cancel culture discourages executives from expressing beliefs or making value judgments. Without connection to purposes beyond profit, organizations drift from addressing society's most important challenges.
My general bias on AI is it is dangerous. There are positive and negative consequences, and either we win or China will win.

Trump has a point about the AI race. There's a real hesitance to adopt these kind of products in the West. Europe and Canada are falling behind. This is about the haves and the have-nots. The countries and companies that adopt AI effectively will be in a fundamentally different position than those that don't.

We want the world's democracies to win. If you don't want that, that's your prerogative, but don't work here. The software we build is used to target adversaries and protect our soldiers. I am proud of that.
source: Palantir earnings call and public statements

content: Burry's shorts on Palantir and Nvidia are batshit crazy. We are the ones making all the money. Betting against Palantir and Nvidia is essentially betting against AI itself, and that's a bet that will not age well.

There are two AI markets and only one actually works. The part of the market we're in — where we can show results quickly and get paid — is expanding, and that part will dominate the rest of the institutional market. The other part, where companies pay for AI they can't deploy in production, is a market that will collapse.

AI will not replace labor workers — it's not true. AI could lead to dysfunction and even violence if benefits aren't spread broadly to workers at all levels. AI gives welders superpowers. The danger zone is AI dysfunction if benefits are unequally distributed.

Do you believe the West? Do you believe the West has created a superior way of living? We are active in defending the values of the West and our belief that the West is a superior way to live. Not only is it right; it will make you rich.

As usual, I've been cautioned to be a little modest. We reported 70% year-over-year revenue growth to $1.407 billion in the fourth quarter and a rule of 40 score of 127. Some AI investments may not create enough value to justify their cost. We reject an idea of software that becomes parasitic — you can't get rid of it and you have 1,000 sales dinners convincing you to buy something useless.

The tech industry currently views government as an obstacle rather than a collaborator. This is a historic betrayal of the relationship that actually built Silicon Valley — DARPA, NASA, the defense establishment. The government should embrace the most effective features of the engineering mindset that has propelled Silicon Valley's success.

Critics of AI surveillance want more AI surveillance for the things they care about, just not the things they don't. There is no principled distinction between using AI to track financial fraud and using it to enforce immigration law. The question is always whether the state is legitimate. And in the United States, it is.

We must preserve space for ideological confrontation. Timid leadership, intellectual fragility, and an unambitious view of technology's potential have made the U.S. vulnerable in an era of mounting global threats. Distributing decision-making authority to those closest to actual problems is not just good management — it is a national security imperative.
